emerald.name: Emerald
emerald.metaphysical: Associated with the heart chakra, emotional balance, loyalty and renewal. Traditionally carried as a stone of successful love.
emerald.care: Never use ultrasonic or steam cleaners — most emeralds are oiled and heat or vibration can disturb the filler. Clean with lukewarm water, mild soap and a soft brush.
emerald.typicalTreatment: Minor to moderate oiling or resin filling of surface-reaching fissures is standard and accepted trade practice.
emerald.buyingNotes: Colour saturation drives value far more than clarity. A slightly included stone with vivid, evenly distributed green outperforms a pale, clean one. Swat Valley (Pakistan) emeralds are prized for a cool, slightly bluish green and often show better transparency than Colombian material at the same price point.
emerald.priceDriver: Colour saturation, transparency, cut quality, origin
faq: Are emeralds with inclusions normal?

Yes. Emeralds are a Type III gemstone, meaning inclusions are expected in virtually every stone. The trade calls the internal landscape the jardin (French for garden). A completely clean emerald of any size is more likely to be synthetic than natural.
What makes Swat emeralds different?

Emeralds from the Swat Valley in Pakistan are coloured by chromium and tend toward a cool, slightly bluish green with strong saturation at small sizes. They are typically cut in Peshawar and reach the market with less oiling than Colombian goods.
Can I wear an emerald ring daily?
With care. At 7.5-8 Mohs an emerald resists scratching, but its internal fissures make it prone to chipping on impact. A bezel or protective setting is recommended for daily wear.

ruby.name: Ruby
ruby.metaphysical: A stone of vitality, courage and passion. Associated with the root chakra and with protective, energising intent.
ruby.care: Extremely durable. Safe for ultrasonic cleaning unless the stone is glass-filled or heavily fractured — glass-filled rubies must only be cleaned with soap and water.
ruby.typicalTreatment: Heat treatment is near-universal and stable. Lead-glass filling is a separate, lower-value treatment that must always be disclosed.
ruby.buyingNotes: Pigeon blood — a vivid pure red with a faint blue undertone and strong fluorescence — is the benchmark grade. Certification from GUILD, GRS or SSEF matters most above 1 carat, where price per carat rises steeply.
ruby.priceDriver: Colour (pigeon blood premium), treatment status, clarity, size
faq: What does pigeon blood ruby mean?

It describes the top colour grade: a vivid, saturated pure red with a slight blue secondary hue and strong red fluorescence under daylight. The term is applied by laboratories such as GUILD and GRS and carries a substantial price premium.
Is a heated ruby still natural?
Yes. Heat treatment mimics geological conditions, is permanent and stable, and applies to the large majority of rubies on the market. Unheated stones of good colour command a significant premium.
What is a glass-filled ruby?

Low-grade corundum whose fractures have been filled with lead glass to improve apparent clarity. It is a legitimate product when disclosed, but it is far less valuable and less durable than heated ruby, and it cannot be cleaned ultrasonically or resized with heat.

sapphire.name: Sapphire
sapphire.metaphysical: Linked to wisdom, clarity of thought and truthful speech. Blue sapphire is worked with at the throat chakra.
sapphire.care: One of the toughest gems available. Ultrasonic and steam safe for untreated and heat-treated stones.
sapphire.typicalTreatment: Heat treatment is standard and stable. Diffusion and beryllium treatment exist and materially reduce value — always require disclosure.
sapphire.buyingNotes: Sapphire is corundum in every colour except red. Velvety cornflower blue carries the highest premium, but yellow and purple sapphires offer far more colour per euro and are dramatically under-priced relative to blue.
sapphire.priceDriver: Colour saturation and evenness, treatment, origin, size
faq: Are sapphires only blue?
No. Sapphire is the name for all gem corundum other than red, which is called ruby. Yellow, purple, pink, green, orange and colourless sapphires are all natural and all correctly called sapphire.
Is sapphire good for an engagement ring?
It is arguably the best non-diamond choice. At Mohs 9 it is second only to diamond in scratch resistance, it has no cleavage plane, and it tolerates daily wear better than emerald, opal or tanzanite.
What is purple corundum?

Purple corundum is purple sapphire. Sellers sometimes use the term corundum when a stone has not been laboratory-certified as sapphire, or to describe material with a colour that sits between pink and purple.

tourmaline.name: Tourmaline
tourmaline.metaphysical: Green and pink tourmaline are heart chakra stones of compassion and emotional repair. Black tourmaline is the classic protection and grounding stone.
tourmaline.care: Warm soapy water only. Avoid ultrasonic cleaning — tourmaline is often included and can fracture. Avoid sudden temperature change.
tourmaline.typicalTreatment: Heat treatment and irradiation are common in pink and red material to improve colour. Usually stable.
tourmaline.buyingNotes: Tourmaline offers the widest colour range of any gem species. Indicolite (blue-teal) and rubellite (true red-pink) carry the strongest premiums. Look for even colour without a dark axis when viewed through the table.
tourmaline.priceDriver: Colour rarity (indicolite and rubellite highest), clarity, size
faq: What is indicolite tourmaline?
Indicolite is the varietal name for blue to blue-green tourmaline. It is significantly rarer than green or pink tourmaline and commands a premium, especially in vivid teal tones without grey masking.
What is rubellite?

Rubellite is intense pink to red tourmaline that holds its colour under both daylight and incandescent light. Stones that shift toward brown or purple in artificial light are correctly sold as pink tourmaline rather than rubellite.
Why is black tourmaline so popular?
Black tourmaline, or schorl, is the most widely used protective and grounding crystal in modern practice. It is also the most abundant tourmaline variety, which keeps specimen prices accessible.

garnet.name: Garnet
garnet.metaphysical: A grounding, energising stone associated with vitality, commitment and safe travel.
garnet.care: Warm soapy water. Generally ultrasonic safe if free of large fractures. Avoid steam.
garnet.typicalTreatment: Garnet is one of the very few gem families that is essentially never treated. What you see is what the earth produced.
garnet.buyingNotes: Garnet is a family, not a single stone. Spessartine (fanta orange), rhodolite (raspberry red-purple) and hessonite (cinnamon orange) are distinct species with different values. Spessartine with true fanta saturation is the most sought after.
faq: Is garnet always red?

No. The garnet family spans orange spessartine and hessonite, raspberry rhodolite, green tsavorite and demantoid, and colour-change varieties. Only almandine and pyrope match the deep red most people picture.
What is spessartine garnet?
Spessartine is the manganese-aluminium garnet responsible for vivid orange to reddish-orange colours. Top material is marketed as mandarin or fanta garnet and is never heat treated.
Is garnet hard enough for a ring?
At 6.5-7.5 Mohs it is suitable for rings with reasonable care. It sits below sapphire and above opal or fluorite in durability, so a protective setting extends its life in daily wear.

peridot.name: Peridot
peridot.metaphysical: A stone of renewal, abundance and release of old patterns. Worked with at the heart and solar plexus.
peridot.care: Sensitive to acids and to rapid temperature change. Clean with mild soap and lukewarm water only. Never ultrasonic.
peridot.typicalTreatment: Essentially never treated. Peridot reaches the market in its natural state.
peridot.buyingNotes: Peridot is idiochromatic — its green comes from iron in the crystal structure itself, so every stone is green and the only question is tone. The finest is a pure grass green without brown or yellow masking, most often from Pakistan.
peridot.priceDriver: Colour purity, size (large clean stones are scarce), clarity
faq: Why does peridot look different under artificial light?
Peridot has a strong yellow-green body colour and low dispersion, so it can appear flatter under warm incandescent light and most vivid in daylight or cool LED. This is normal and not a fault.
Is peridot a soft stone?
At 6.5-7 Mohs it is moderately durable but has distinct sensitivity to acid and thermal shock. It suits earrings and pendants better than everyday rings unless protectively set.
Where does the best peridot come from?
Pakistan's Kohistan region produces the largest and cleanest peridot in the world, including stones over 20 carats with exceptional colour. Arizona and Myanmar are the other significant sources.

topaz.name: Topaz
topaz.metaphysical: Blue topaz is a throat chakra stone of clear communication. Imperial and golden topaz are associated with confidence and personal power.
topaz.care: Topaz has perfect basal cleavage — a sharp knock in the wrong direction can split it regardless of its hardness. Never ultrasonic or steam. Avoid prolonged strong light for pink material.
topaz.typicalTreatment: Blue topaz is almost always irradiated and heated colourless topaz. The treatment is permanent and the material is safe. Imperial topaz is naturally coloured.
topaz.buyingNotes: Sky blue, Swiss blue and London blue describe increasing depth of colour, not different species. Imperial topaz — the natural golden to peach-pink material — is far rarer than any blue topaz and priced accordingly.
topaz.priceDriver: Natural vs treated colour, size, cut precision
faq: Is blue topaz natural?

The material is natural topaz, but the blue colour is nearly always produced by irradiating and heating colourless topaz. The colour is permanent and the stones are safe to wear. Naturally blue topaz exists but is pale and commercially rare.
What is imperial topaz?
Imperial topaz is the naturally golden-orange to pinkish material, historically from Ouro Preto in Brazil. It is not irradiated, is far scarcer than blue topaz, and holds its value much better.
Why does topaz chip if it is hardness 8?

Hardness measures scratch resistance, not toughness. Topaz has one direction of perfect cleavage, so an impact aligned with that plane can cleave the stone cleanly even though it resists scratching very well.

amethyst.name: Amethyst
amethyst.metaphysical: The classic stone of calm, sobriety and spiritual clarity. Used at the third eye and crown for meditation and sleep.
amethyst.care: Durable and easy. Warm soapy water. Keep out of prolonged direct sunlight, which can fade deep purple over years.
amethyst.typicalTreatment: Usually untreated. Heating amethyst produces citrine and prasiolite (green amethyst), and this is disclosed as treated material.
amethyst.buyingNotes: Value sits almost entirely in colour. Deep Siberian purple with red flashes leads; pale or zoned material is abundant and inexpensive. Size adds little premium because large clean amethyst is common.
amethyst.priceDriver: Colour depth and evenness, absence of zoning
faq: What is green amethyst?

Green amethyst, correctly called prasiolite, is amethyst that has been heat treated to a soft leek-green. Natural prasiolite exists but is extremely rare, so nearly all material on the market is treated and should be sold as such.
Does amethyst fade?
Deeply coloured amethyst can fade with years of intense sunlight exposure. Ordinary wear and indoor light pose no risk. Store display pieces out of a sunny window.
Why is amethyst so affordable?
Enormous deposits in Brazil, Uruguay and Zambia supply the market with high-quality material in large sizes. Scarcity, not beauty, sets gem prices — and amethyst is simply not scarce.

citrine.name: Citrine
citrine.metaphysical: Known as the merchant's stone — associated with abundance, confidence and manifestation. Worked with at the solar plexus.
citrine.care: Easy to live with. Warm soapy water. Avoid prolonged high heat, which can shift colour.
citrine.typicalTreatment: Much commercial citrine is heat-treated amethyst. Natural citrine tends toward a paler, smokier yellow; deep orange-red material is usually treated.
citrine.buyingNotes: Honey and Madeira tones command more than pale lemon. Because large clean crystals are common, citrine is one of the best values in gemstones for statement-sized stones above 10 carats.
citrine.priceDriver: Colour depth (honey/Madeira premium), size for statement pieces
faq: Is my citrine natural or heated amethyst?

Most deep orange citrine on the market is heat-treated amethyst, and this is standard, permanent and accepted. Natural citrine is usually a lighter, slightly smoky yellow. Neither is a lesser stone, but disclosure matters for collectors.
What is the difference between honey and lemon citrine?
These are colour descriptions, not species. Honey citrine is a warm golden-brown, lemon is a bright pale yellow, and Madeira is a deep reddish-orange. Honey and Madeira are generally more valuable.
Can citrine be worn every day?
Yes. At Mohs 7 with no cleavage, citrine handles daily wear well. It is one of the more practical choices for a large statement ring.

aquamarine.name: Aquamarine
aquamarine.metaphysical: A stone of calm, courage and clear expression. Traditionally the sailor's protective stone.
aquamarine.care: Durable and ultrasonic-safe when free of fractures. Warm soapy water is sufficient.
aquamarine.typicalTreatment: Routinely heated to remove green and yellow tones, producing purer blue. The treatment is permanent and undetectable.
aquamarine.buyingNotes: Unlike emerald, aquamarine is usually eye-clean, so clarity is assumed and colour carries the price. Deeper Santa Maria blue is worth several times a pale sky tone, and larger stones show colour better.
aquamarine.priceDriver: Colour depth, size (colour intensifies with size), clarity
faq: Why is aquamarine usually pale?

Aquamarine's colouring agent, iron, produces a light tone in small sizes. Colour deepens visibly above roughly 5 carats, which is why large aquamarines look far more saturated than small ones cut from the same rough.
Is aquamarine the same as blue topaz?
No. Aquamarine is beryl, related to emerald, and its blue is natural or heat-refined. Blue topaz is a different mineral whose colour comes from irradiation. Aquamarine is considerably more valuable.
Is aquamarine good for engagement rings?
Yes, at 7.5-8 Mohs it is durable enough for regular wear, though it is softer than sapphire and shows scratches over decades. A protective setting is sensible.

quartz.name: Quartz
quartz.metaphysical: Clear quartz is the master amplifier and programmable stone. Smoky quartz grounds and clears dense energy.
quartz.care: Very easy. Warm soapy water. Smoky quartz colour can fade with intense prolonged sunlight.
quartz.typicalTreatment: Clear quartz is untreated. Some smoky quartz is irradiated to deepen colour.
quartz.buyingNotes: Quartz is valued for size, clarity and crystal form rather than rarity. For specimens, an undamaged termination and good matrix presentation matter more than weight.
quartz.priceDriver: Crystal form and termination quality, clarity, size
faq: What makes a good clear quartz specimen?
An undamaged point, good transparency, and an attractive relationship between crystal and matrix. Chips on the termination reduce value sharply even on large pieces.
Is smoky quartz irradiated?

Some is. Natural smoky quartz forms through geological irradiation of aluminium-bearing quartz over millions of years; commercial treatment simply accelerates the same process, and the result is stable and safe.
Why is quartz used in so many crystal practices?
It is abundant, forms in clear well-terminated crystals, is hard enough to handle daily, and is inexpensive at collector sizes — a rare combination that made it the default working stone.

spinel.name: Spinel
spinel.metaphysical: A stone of revitalisation and endurance, associated with renewed energy after depletion.
spinel.care: Durable and ultrasonic-safe. Warm soapy water.
spinel.typicalTreatment: Almost always completely untreated, which is a significant part of its appeal to collectors.
spinel.buyingNotes: Spinel spent centuries misidentified as ruby — several famous crown rubies are spinel. It is singly refractive, brilliant, untreated and still under-priced relative to corundum, making it the connoisseur's value pick.
spinel.priceDriver: Colour (hot pink and red highest), clarity, size, untreated status
faq: Is spinel a real gemstone?

Very much so. Spinel is a distinct natural mineral, not a synthetic or a substitute. The confusion comes from historic misidentification — the Black Prince's Ruby in the British Imperial State Crown is in fact a spinel.
Why is spinel cheaper than ruby?

Purely market recognition. Spinel is rarer than ruby in fine qualities, is almost never treated, and is nearly as hard, but it lacks ruby's centuries of name recognition — which is exactly why collectors buy it.
Is spinel treated?
Rarely. The overwhelming majority of spinel on the market is entirely untreated, in sharp contrast to ruby and sapphire where heat treatment is near-universal.

fluorite.name: Fluorite
fluorite.metaphysical: Known as the genius stone — associated with mental order, focus and clearing confusion.
fluorite.care: Soft and cleavable in four directions. Handle as a collector or display stone. Never ultrasonic. Keep from knocks and from direct sunlight.
fluorite.typicalTreatment: Untreated. Some material is coated, which should be disclosed.
fluorite.buyingNotes: At Mohs 4 fluorite is a display and pendant stone rather than a ring stone. Its appeal is colour banding and the fact that it can be cut in very large sizes with remarkable clarity for a fraction of the cost of any hard gem.
fluorite.priceDriver: Colour zoning and vividness, size, cut quality
faq: Can fluorite be set in a ring?

It is not recommended for daily wear. At Mohs 4 with perfect octahedral cleavage, fluorite scratches against ordinary dust and can cleave on impact. It performs best in pendants, earrings and display settings.
Why is fluorite so inexpensive for its size?
Fluorite forms in large, clean, well-coloured crystals in many localities. Its softness limits jewellery demand, so beautiful 15-20 carat stones remain affordable.
Does fluorite glow?
Many fluorites fluoresce blue or violet under UV light — the phenomenon of fluorescence is literally named after this mineral.

corundum.name: Corundum
corundum.metaphysical: Purple corundum bridges the heart and higher mind, associated with insight and calm discernment.
corundum.care: Extremely durable at Mohs 9. Ultrasonic and steam safe.
corundum.typicalTreatment: Commonly heat treated. Disclosure of diffusion treatment is essential.
corundum.buyingNotes: Purple corundum is purple sapphire under a more cautious trade name. The material offers Mohs 9 durability and genuine rarity at a fraction of blue sapphire pricing.
faq: Is purple corundum the same as purple sapphire?

Chemically and gemmologically, yes — both are gem-quality aluminium oxide. Sellers sometimes prefer corundum when a stone has not been lab-certified, so the term often signals caution rather than a lesser stone.
Is purple corundum rare?
Fine purple corundum is scarcer than blue sapphire in comparable qualities, but it attracts far less demand, which keeps prices well below what its rarity would otherwise justify.
How durable is it?
Identical to ruby and sapphire — Mohs 9, no cleavage, excellent toughness. It is among the most wearable coloured stones available.

ametrine.name: Ametrine
ametrine.metaphysical: Unites amethyst's calm with citrine's drive — associated with balanced decision-making and creative follow-through.
ametrine.care: Warm soapy water. Avoid prolonged sunlight and sudden heat, which can blur the colour boundary.
ametrine.typicalTreatment: Natural ametrine is untreated. Synthetic and heat-zoned imitations exist, so provenance matters.
ametrine.buyingNotes: Virtually all natural ametrine comes from the Anahí mine in Bolivia. Value rests on a crisp, well-centred boundary between purple and gold, and on a cut that presents both colours evenly through the table.
ametrine.priceDriver: Sharpness and placement of the colour boundary, size, cut orientation
faq: What causes the two colours in ametrine?
Different oxidation states of iron in adjacent growth sectors of the same crystal, formed under a natural temperature gradient. One sector becomes amethyst, the neighbouring one citrine.
Where does ametrine come from?
Nearly all commercial natural ametrine originates from the Anahí mine in eastern Bolivia. Material claiming other origins warrants scrutiny.
How should ametrine be cut?
The best cutting places the colour boundary along the centre of the stone so both colours read equally face-up. Emerald and rectangular cuts show the division most cleanly.

beryl.name: Beryl
beryl.metaphysical: The parent species of emerald and aquamarine, associated with clarity of purpose and emotional steadiness.
beryl.care: Clean with mild soap and lukewarm water. Avoid ultrasonic cleaning on included or fissured material.
beryl.typicalTreatment: Varies by variety — emerald is oiled, aquamarine heated, and specimen-grade beryl is generally untreated.
beryl.buyingNotes: Specimen beryl is bought for crystal form, termination quality and matrix presentation rather than for facetable clarity.
beryl.priceDriver: Crystal form, colour, matrix presentation, locality
faq: What is beryl?

Beryl is the mineral species that produces emerald (green, chromium-coloured), aquamarine (blue), morganite (pink), heliodor (yellow) and goshenite (colourless). The variety name depends entirely on the trace element present.
Is a beryl specimen the same as an emerald?
Only if it is green from chromium or vanadium and of gem quality. Green beryl below that saturation threshold is correctly sold as green beryl rather than emerald.
What makes a good matrix specimen?
Undamaged crystal terminations, good colour contrast against the host rock, and a stable natural presentation that displays well without artificial repair.
